text: My name is Anne Defnit, and I'm pleased to present a case of robotic transversus abdominis muscle release for recurrent substernal incisional hernia. We have nothing to disclose. The patient is a seventy-five-year-old male, BMI twenty-seven, who was referred for recurrent substernal incisional hernia. His past surgical history includes coronary artery bypass grafting in two thousand six, as well as two prior substernal hernia repairs, both onlay repairs with polypropylene mesh. CT abdomen pelvis was obtained, revealing a seven centimeter defect with herniated colon. His physical exam mirrored his imaging findings.

Three 8mm ports are placed at the level of the anterior superior iliac spine, and the XI da Vinci robot is docked. Upon entry into the abdomen, the hernia and its contents are visible. The surgeon begins dissecting the retrorectus space by incising the posterior sheath 10 centimeters below the hernia defect, freeing it from the underlying muscles. The dissection extends to the contralateral side, crossing the midline, and exposes the linea alba and bilateral rectus abdominis muscles. The dissection continues superiorly towards the hernia defect.

The surgeon dissects the hernia contents free from the defect, leaving the sac adherent to the polypropylene mesh. The rectus sheath is excised from the fascial defect edges. The retrorectus space is dissected laterally to expose the transversus abdominis muscle, which is transected with electrocautery, revealing the underlying transversalis fascia.
The surgeon exposes the diaphragm superiorly, carefully dissecting it free from the transversalis fascia. The dissection continues inferiorly, ensuring preservation of all neurovascular bundles.

Following bilateral transversus abdominis release, the posterior sheath is fully freed from the anterior abdominal wall, revealing the diaphragm. The surgeon closes the anterior rectus fascial defect with a two-layer 2-0 reverse barbed suture, noting residual tension, particularly superiorly. The closure includes bites of the hernia sac to prevent seroma formation. The posterior rectus sheath is closed in the midline with 2-0 reverse barbed suture without tension. An 18x18 cm composite mesh, containing PTFE and bioabsorbable components, is placed in the retro-rectus space and secured to the anterior abdominal wall with 3-0 suture. The posterior rectus sheath is then closed transversely with 2-0 reverse barbed suture, completing the procedure.

The patient recovered without a vent and was discharged post-op day one. CT abdomen pelvis was performed one year post-op or follow-up, revealing an intact repair without recurrence with mesh apparent in the retrorectus space.
